front: What is the kernel (or null space) of a linear transformation T: V → W?
back: The kernel of T is the set of all vectors in V that map to the zero vector in W. Formally, Ker(T) = { v ∈ V | T(v) = 0_W }.

front: What is the image (or range) of a linear transformation T: V → W?
back: The image of T is the set of all vectors in W that are the output of T for some input vector in V. Formally, Im(T) = { w ∈ W | there exists v ∈ V such that T(v) = w }.

front: State the Rank-Nullity Theorem for a linear transformation T: V → W, where V is finite-dimensional.
back: dim(Ker(T)) + dim(Im(T)) = dim(V). The dimension of the kernel (nullity) plus the dimension of the image (rank) equals the dimension of the domain vector space V.

front: What important property does the kernel of a linear transformation possess?
back: The kernel is a subspace of the domain V. It is closed under vector addition and scalar multiplication because T is a linear map.

front: How is the injectivity (one-to-one property) of a linear transformation T characterized using its kernel?
back: A linear transformation T is injective (one-to-one) if and only if its kernel contains only the zero vector. That is, Ker(T) = { 0_V }.
